Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic this semester. I tried to set aside time in the mornings since I'm more alert then, but with 20 online courses, I was constantly jumping between different subjects. I'd create visual schedules and color-coded calendars that looked beautiful but rarely followed them consistently. Most of my actual studying happened in intense bursts when I felt the pressure mounting, which wasn't ideal but it's how I coped.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly depending on what was due and how anxious I was feeling on any given day. Some weeks I'd be completely consistent, studying every morning like clockwork. Then something would throw me off—an extracurricular commitment, a bad anxiety day, or just feeling overwhelmed—and I'd fall off track completely. Deadlines definitely dictated when I'd cram versus when I'd take it easier.
Q3: Usually it was panic, if I'm being honest. I'd suddenly remember an assignment or realize I hadn't checked announcements in days, and that spike of anxiety would send me straight to the LMS. Sometimes my peer support group would message me reminding me about something, and that would prompt me to log in. Occasionally I'd log in just to feel like I was doing something productive, even if I didn't accomplish much.
Q4: There was a brief period mid-semester where logging in every morning with my coffee became routine. My peer support buddy and I had agreed to check in on the discussion boards together, which helped make it feel less lonely. But that habit fell apart when my anxiety got really bad and I started avoiding anything course-related. The routine never fully stuck because my mental state was so unpredictable.
Q5: The video lectures were honestly a lifesaver for me as a visual learner—I watched almost 189 minutes of content because seeing concepts explained visually helped them click. The discussion boards were also valuable because connecting with peers made me feel less isolated in my struggles. When other students shared their confusion, it normalized my own difficulties and sometimes their explanations made more sense than the official materials.
Q6: I definitely avoided the reading-heavy PDFs and text-based resources. They just didn't work for my brain, and I'd find myself reading the same paragraph five times without absorbing anything. I also minimally used the practice quizzes even though I knew they'd help—something about them triggered my anxiety about failing. It felt safer to avoid them than to confirm I didn't understand the material.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see immediate progress or when it connected to something I cared about. Visual content that I could engage with and actually understand made me feel like I was learning, not just going through motions. Discussions with peers also felt valuable because the human connection reminded me why I was doing this. If something felt like busywork with no clear purpose, I'd struggle to prioritize it.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford, honestly. With so many required tasks already overwhelming me, optional content seemed like something for students who had their lives together. There was also less guilt attached to skipping them, which was a relief. Required activities came with this heavy weight of obligation and fear of consequences that optional ones didn't carry.
Q9: Absolutely, I did a lot of studying that never showed up in the system. I'd download lecture slides and annotate them on my tablet while sitting in cafes because the LMS interface stressed me out. I also used YouTube videos to supplement confusing topics and created visual mind maps in my notebook. My peer support friends and I would meet in person to study together, which was way more helpful than any online interaction.
Q10: So many times! I'd screenshot diagrams and study them on my phone during commutes or while waiting for things. I'd spend hours with my handwritten notes, reorganizing concepts visually in ways that helped me understand them. There were whole study sessions at the library with my peer group where we'd work through problems together on whiteboards. None of that showed up anywhere in the LMS data.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd watch a video once online, then download the slides and never return to the LMS version. My tablet was full of course materials that I'd access offline when I needed to avoid the anxiety of logging into the system. Sometimes just seeing the LMS interface with all its notifications and deadlines would send my stress through the roof, so working offline felt safer.
Q12: There was a period about two-thirds through the semester when everything crashed for me. My anxiety was at its peak, I was behind in multiple courses, and I just couldn't face logging in anymore. The weight of seeing how much I'd missed felt paralyzing. My attendance dropped to almost nothing during that time, and I relied entirely on my peer support network to tell me what was absolutely critical.
Q13: The workload was genuinely unmanageable with 20 courses—I don't know what I was thinking. Confusion about expectations made everything worse because I'd spend hours trying to figure out what was even required. When fatigue hit, I'd log in, stare at the screen, and accomplish nothing. My extracurricular commitments actually helped my mental health, but they also competed for the limited energy I had for academics.
Q14: More times than I'd like to admit. I'd have the LMS open, videos playing, but my mind would be somewhere else entirely—worrying about other deadlines or just dissociating from the stress. Sometimes I'd click through materials just to see the completion checkmarks without actually processing anything. It was like going through the motions to feel less guilty about not really engaging.
Q15: I postponed exams and assignments constantly, even when I knew they were important. The anxiety of potentially failing made starting feel impossible—like if I didn't try, I couldn't really fail. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I felt better, but that better day rarely came. It's a terrible cycle where avoidance increases anxiety which increases avoidance.
Q16: Deadlines were both my enemy and my only motivator. Without them, I might never have submitted anything, but they also caused intense stress spirals. I'd cram everything into the last possible hours, which meant my work quality suffered even when I understood the material. The LMS deadline notifications became a source of dread rather than helpful reminders.
Q17: When I actually got feedback, it could go either way. Positive feedback motivated me to engage more with that course specifically. But critical feedback, even constructive criticism, could send me into an avoidance spiral where I wouldn't log in for days. My peer support group helped me process feedback more healthily, but it was still emotionally difficult to receive.
Q18: The course structure felt overwhelming with so much content spread across the LMS. I needed more visual roadmaps showing how everything connected, but instead it felt like a maze of modules. The flexibility of online learning was supposed to help, but without external structure, my anxiety made it hard to create my own. I thrived more when there were clear, simple pathways to follow.
Q19: Having videos and visual content made engagement easier for sure. But the sheer volume of materials and the complicated navigation made everything harder. Discussion boards were helpful when active, but sometimes they felt dead and lonely. I wish there had been more structured peer collaboration built into the design rather than just optional forums.
Q20: That was literally me this semester. I'd log in frequently, watch videos, browse through materials, participate in discussions—but then freeze when it came to actually submitting work. The assignments felt like high-stakes judgments of my worth, while browsing felt safe. Sometimes high activity is just anxiety manifesting as motion without progress.
Q21: I can see how that happens—some students are just efficient and confident. They download what they need, study offline, and submit strong work without needing constant LMS interaction. I wish I could be like that, but my anxiety made me need the reassurance of checking in frequently, even though it didn't translate to better performance. Different students have different relationships with the platform.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns of when you're active and what you click on, which reveals something about habits and engagement timing. But they completely miss the quality of that engagement—whether you're actually learning or just anxiously clicking around. They miss offline studying, peer collaboration, emotional state, and whether someone is genuinely processing material or just going through motions.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about and connecting with the material, not just completing tasks. It's when I'm curious, asking questions, making connections to other things I know. For me, real engagement often happened in conversations with peers or when I was drawing diagrams to understand concepts. It's an internal state that doesn't always match external activity.
Q24: The LMS would never show how much my peer support network carried me through this semester—those human connections were everything. It wouldn't show my anxiety attacks before logging in, or the hours I spent studying offline because the platform stressed me out. It wouldn't capture that despite failing, I actually learned things and grew as a person. The data tells a story of failure, but my experience was more complicated than that.
